title: Why good news can trigger a panic response
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
It happened again, didn't it? 

 You're sitting there, maybe scrolling your phone or talking to a friend. Someone shares something genuinely good. A promotion....
metaDescription:
It happened again, didn't it? 

 You're sitting there, maybe scrolling your phone or talking to a friend. Someone shares something genuinely good. A promo...
htmlContent:
It happened again, didn't it?

You're sitting there, maybe scrolling your phone or talking to a friend. Someone shares something genuinely good. A promotion. A pregnancy. A clean bill of health. And instead of feeling happy, your chest tightens. Your throat closes. Your heart starts slamming against your ribs like it's trying to escape.

What the hell is that about?

Here's the thing. I've been there too. More times than I can count. A partner tells me they love me and I feel like I'm going to throw up. A boss gives me unexpected praise and I spend the next hour waiting for the other shoe to drop. Good news lands in my lap and my nervous system treats it like a threat.

It makes no sense on the surface. But your nervous system isn't operating on logic. It's operating on survival. And for those of us with complex trauma, survival means staying hypervigilant for danger. Even when danger isn't there.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would say your body is stuck in a defense state. Your vagus nerve, that long wandering bundle of nerves connecting your brain to your organs, has learned that safety is temporary. That good moments are just setups for betrayal. That letting your guard down means getting hurt.

So when something good happens, your system doesn't relax. It braces. It scans for the hidden threat. It asks: What's the catch? And when it can't find one, it panics anyway. Because panic is familiar. Panic is predictable. Panic keeps you alive.

Your nervous system learned this for a reason

I'm not saying this to make you feel broken. I'm saying it because understanding the why matters. Your brain didn't misfire. It adapted. It learned that good news was often followed by something bad. Maybe you grew up in a house where a parent's mood could flip in an instant. Where a compliment was weaponized later. Where joy was punished because it made you too visible.

Something I wish someone had told me earlier. You can be triggered by good things too. Safety can be a trigger. Love can be a trigger. Success can be a trigger. Because if your nervous system learned that good things are always followed by bad things, then good things become the warning sign.

This is what Pete Walker calls "abandonment melange" - that toxic cocktail of fear, shame, and grief that floods the system when intimacy gets too close. Your body learned that closeness equals danger. That vulnerability equals pain. So when someone offers you genuine love, your system sounds the alarm. Not because love is dangerous now. But because it was dangerous then.

Sit with that for a moment. If you've ever sabotaged a good relationship, pushed away someone who was kind to you, or felt inexplicable panic when things were going well - this might be why. It's not self-destruction. It's self-protection. Your system is trying to get ahead of the inevitable disappointment. The problem is, the disappointment isn't inevitable anymore. But your body doesn't know that yet. Teaching it the difference takes time, patience, and a willingness to stay when every fiber of your being is screaming "run."

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. Not as a memory you can think your way out of, but as a physical response pattern. Your muscles, your breath, your gut. They remember. They remember that the last time you felt hopeful, something terrible happened. So they do their job. They protect you from hope.

Think about it. When good news arrives, your body might respond with:

A racing heart. Shallow breathing. Nausea. A feeling of unreality or dissociation. Irritability or anger. An urge to change the subject or leave the room. A compulsion to find something wrong. A sudden exhaustion that makes you want to sleep for three days.

I've felt all of these. The anger one really gets me. Someone gives me a gift and I want to snap at them. Not because I'm ungrateful. Because my system is screaming: This is a trap. They want something. You'll pay for this later.

It's exhausting. And it's lonely. Because the people around you don't get it. They think you're being dramatic or ungrateful or weird. They say things like "Just be happy!" or "Why can't you accept a compliment?" And you want to explain that it's not about the compliment. It's about the fact that your body thinks the compliment is a prelude to a punch.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It walks through recognizing these patterns without making you feel like you're broken for having them.

What's actually happening inside your body

Let's get nerdy for a second. Stay with me here. Because understanding the mechanics helps me feel less crazy when it happens.

Your autonomic nervous system has two main branches. The sympathetic branch is your accelerator. It's the fight or flight system. It revs you up for action. The parasympathetic branch is your brake. It's the rest and digest system. It calms you down.

But here's where it gets complicated. Your vagus nerve has two distinct pathways. The ventral vagal pathway is the "smart" vagus. It's connected to your face, your voice, your heart, and your social engagement system. It lets you feel safe with other people. It lets you connect, laugh, and rest. The dorsal vagal pathway is the older, more primitive vagus. It's the freeze response. It shuts you down when things get too overwhelming.

For people with trauma histories, the ventral vagal pathway often gets bypassed. Your system jumps straight from sympathetic activation (panic) to dorsal collapse (numbness) without ever touching safety. Good news triggers the sympathetic response because your brain predicts that safety is dangerous. It's safer to stay activated and vigilant than to risk being caught off guard.

Dan Siegel, the psychiatrist who coined the term "window of tolerance," describes this as your window narrowing. When you're traumatized, your window for what you can handle without dysregulation gets smaller. Good news pushes you out of that window just as easily as bad news does. Because both require you to feel something intensely. And intense feeling, regardless of the flavor, can feel threatening when your system has learned that feelings lead to pain.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The deep pressure touch stimulation can help signal safety to your body, especially when good news sends you into that jittery, panicked state.

What helps when good news triggers you

I'm not going to tell you to just breathe through it. That's wellness industry garbage that ignores the reality of a dysregulated nervous system. When your system is screaming DANGER, telling it to take a deep breath is like telling a house fire to just cool down.

I want to talk about something that most people don't understand about triggers. They're not just emotional. They're physical. Your body doesn't just "feel upset" when triggered - it launches a full physiological cascade. Cortisol spikes. Heart rate increases. Blood pressure rises. Digestion shuts down. Peripheral vision narrows. Your body is preparing for a threat that isn't there anymore.

This is why "just calm down" doesn't work. You can't talk yourself out of a physiological state. It's like telling someone having an asthma attack to "just breathe normally." The system is doing what it's doing. You have to work with it, not against it.

Deb Dana's work on the polyvagal ladder gives us a map for this. When you're triggered, you've dropped down the ladder - from ventral vagal (safe, social) to sympathetic (fight/flight) or dorsal vagal (freeze/collapse). The way back up isn't through the mind. It's through the body. Through breath. Through movement. Through co-regulation with a safe other. Through tiny, repeated experiences of "I survived that. I'm still here. The danger passed."

But there are things that help. Small things. Practical things. Things that respect that your body is doing its job, even if that job is inconvenient as hell.

First, name it out loud. Say to yourself or to someone you trust: "I'm having a panic response to good news right now." Just naming it changes the relationship to it. You're not broken. You're responding to a perceived threat. Your body is trying to protect you. Thank it for trying. Then tell it you've got this.

Second, slow the exhale. Not the inhale. The exhale. Your inhale activates the sympathetic system. Your exhale activates the parasympathetic. Make your exhale longer than your inhale. Breathe in for four counts, out for six. Or in for four, out for eight. Whatever works. Do it for a minute. Notice if anything shifts.

A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). It gives you a physical timer so you don't have to count in your head. Sometimes that external structure helps when your mind is spinning.

Third, ground yourself in the present. Panic responses to good news are almost always about the past. Your body is reacting to something that happened before, not what's happening now. Bring yourself back. Feel your feet on the floor. Name five things you can see. Touch something with texture. Your body needs proof that it's safe right now, in this moment.

Fourth, give yourself permission to not feel good. This is the big one. You don't have to be happy about good news. You don't have to perform gratitude. You can just be neutral. You can say "Thank you" and then change the subject. You can take a walk. You can go to bed early. You can tell yourself: "I'm allowed to feel whatever I feel about this. My feelings don't have to make sense to anyone else."

Wild, right? The permission to not feel good is often what lets the panic settle. Because the panic isn't about the good news itself. It's about the pressure to respond correctly. The pressure to perform happiness. The pressure to pretend you're fine when you're not.

This is a process, not a fix

I've been working on this for years. And I still get triggered by good news sometimes. Last week, a friend told me they were proud of me and I felt like I wanted to crawl out of my skin. I didn't. I sat with it. I breathed. I named it. And after a few minutes, the panic faded.

It didn't go away completely. But it faded enough that I could say "Thank you" and mean it. That's a win. Not a perfect win. But a real one.

Your nervous system isn't going to rewire overnight. It took years to learn these patterns. It will take time to unlearn them. But every time you sit with the discomfort instead of running from it, you're teaching your body something new. You're showing it that good news doesn't always lead to pain. That safety is possible. That you can handle whatever comes next.

Be patient with yourself. This is hard work. And you're doing it anyway.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Bessel van der Kolk's research on how trauma reshapes the brain and body is foundational for understanding why your nervous system responds the way it does.

You're not broken. You're adapting. And you can adapt again. One breath at a time.
